title: Meta to put AI chip into production in September as it looks to double computing capacity, memo shows
gist: Meta plans to begin production of an in-house AI accelerator code-named Iris (part of its MTIA program) in September 2026 to support doubling compute capacity to 14 GW next year, signaling that reducing dependence on Nvidia is central to its long-term AI strategy.

insights.claim: Meta plans to begin producing a new in-house AI chip code-named Iris in September 2026, part of its broader MTIA program aimed at reducing dependence on outside suppliers and improving AI workload efficiency.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: A concrete production timeline for Meta's custom accelerator is a near-term demand-shift signal for merchant AI silicon suppliers; watch September for tape-out/ramp confirmation.
insights.quote: the chip is code-named Iris and is part of Meta’s broader MTIA program, which aims to reduce dependence on outside suppliers
insights.locator: Meta Platforms plans to begin producing

insights.claim: Meta's new chip is meant to support a goal of doubling the company's computing capacity to 14 gigawatts next year, reflecting the power and hardware demands of generative AI, recommendation, advertising, and model training.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: A 14 GW capacity target quantifies Meta's hyperscale power/hardware buildout, a bullish tell for data-center power, cooling, and interconnect suppliers.
insights.quote: The chip is expected to support Meta’s goal of doubling its computing capacity to 14 gigawatts next year.
insights.locator: The chip is expected to support

insights.claim: If successful, Meta's Iris chip could help the company control costs and secure capacity in a market where advanced AI chips remain expensive and supply-constrained.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Custom silicon is Meta's lever against constrained, high-cost merchant GPUs; success would pressure Nvidia's pricing power over time.
insights.locator: If successful, Iris could help Meta

insights.claim: Meta is expected to keep relying on Nvidia and other suppliers for years to meet its largest AI training needs, because custom chip programs are difficult to execute.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Tempers the bear case for Nvidia: hyperscaler in-house chips displace inference/efficiency workloads first, not leading-edge training demand.
insights.quote: Meta will likely continue relying on Nvidia and other suppliers for years to meet the biggest training needs.
insights.locator: Still, custom chip programs are difficult

insights.claim: The September production plan signals Meta views chip independence as central to its long-term AI strategy rather than merely a cost-saving experiment.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Frames Meta's silicon effort as strategic vertical integration alongside Google, Microsoft and Amazon custom chips, a structural headwind for merchant accelerator TAM.
insights.quote: Meta sees chip independence as central to its long-term AI strategy, not just a cost-saving experiment
